results.possibleIdentity: Peter Gabriel Neumann (born 1932) is a renowned American computer scientist and researcher who has made significant contributions to computer security, reliability, and risk management. He spent a decade at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) in Murray Hill, New Jersey, from 1960 to 1970, where he was a key figure in the development of the Multics operating system, specifically co-designing its file system with Bob Daley. His technical expertise spans computers, information systems, and programming. In 1971, he joined SRI International in Menlo Park, California, where he continues to serve as a Senior Principal Scientist. Neumann is most widely known as the moderator of the ACM RISKS Forum (RISKS Digest), an online dialogue he founded in 1985 to discuss the impacts of computer-related risks on society. He is also the author of the influential book 'Computer-Related Risks' (1995). Throughout his career, Neumann has shown an interest in the intersection of technology and the arts; he was involved in early generative music research at Harvard in 1957 and authored a notable 1969 paper titled 'The Role of Motherhood in the Pop Art of System Programming.' His presence at Bell Labs during the late 1960s coincides with the formation and peak activity of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which was frequently supported by Bell Labs engineers and scientists.
